threats.title: GaugeCL.notifyRewardAmount: Return Variable Shadows Storage rewardRate, Causing Loss of Remaining Rewards
threats.hypothesis_description: In GaugeCL.notifyRewardAmount, the return variable `uint256 rewardRate` shadows the storage variable `uint256 public rewardRate`. Because return variables are initialized to 0, the `else` branch calculates `leftover = remaining * rewardRate` using the return variable (0), not the storage value from the prior call. The storage variable is never written. This means leftover is always 0, so if notifyRewardAmount is called mid-period, remaining rewards from the prior notification are silently lost and the rate is incorrect.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable code defect in the reward rate calculation. Each epoch's notify call that occurs before _periodFinish will compute leftover=0 instead of the actual remaining rewards, causing those rewards to be effectively destroyed. The tokens are transferred to AlgebraEternalFarming but with an incorrectly calculated rate.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Return variable `uint256 rewardRate` shadows storage variable `uint256 public rewardRate`; in else branch `leftover = remaining * rewardRate` reads the default-0 return variable, not storage

preconditions: notifyRewardAmount called when block.timestamp < _periodFinish (mid-period notification)
Prior call set a non-zero rewardRate that should have remaining leftover

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Function return variable shadows storage variable of same name
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: rewardRate in function signature `returns (..., uint256 rewardRate, ...)` shadows `uint256 public rewardRate` storage. Line 112: `uint256 leftover = remaining * rewardRate` reads return var (0), not storage. Storage rewardRate is NEVER written.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No explicit write to storage rewardRate; no safeguard against shadowing

analysis_questions: How frequently is notifyRewardAmount called mid-period vs at/after period end?
What is the cumulative value of lost leftover rewards across epochs?
Does AlgebraEternalFarming.addRewards still receive the full reward amount even though rate is wrong?
validation_requirements: Confirm return variable shadows storage via compiler output
Calculate leftover loss for realistic mid-period notification scenario
Verify if setRates is called with incorrect rate and quantify impact

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Every time distributeAll is called when block.timestamp < _periodFinish for a CL gauge, the remaining undistributed rewards from the previous notification are not accounted for in the new rate calculation. The tokens are sent to farming but the rate is lower than it should be, causing reward distribution discrepancy.
prerequisites: CL gauge exists and receives emissions
Multiple notify calls within same DURATION period

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Systematic loss of emission rewards for all CL gauge liquidity providers each epoch where mid-period notification occurs
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Lost rewards cannot be recovered; they sit in farming contract at incorrect rate

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying rewardRate storage persistence across multiple notifyRewardAmount calls

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Every epoch where distribution happens before _periodFinish
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable via comparing expected vs actual reward rates on-chain

threats.title: BribeFactoryV3.addRewardsToBribes Inner Loop Uses Outer Array Length, Causing Revert or Missed Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: In addRewardsToBribes, the inner loop iterates `k < _token.length` (outer array length) but accesses `_token[i][k]`. If inner arrays have fewer elements than the outer array, the function reverts with out-of-bounds. If they have more, tokens beyond index `_token.length - 1` are silently skipped.
threats.investigation_rationale: Clear indexing bug: inner loop bound should be `_token[i].length` not `_token.length`. The function is effectively unusable for most legitimate inputs.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Inner loop uses `k < _token.length` (outer dimension) instead of `k < _token[i].length` (inner dimension); accesses `_token[i][k]`

preconditions: BRIBE_ADMIN or owner calls addRewardsToBribes with varying inner array lengths

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: _token.length is the outer array length; should be _token[i].length for inner iteration
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No bounds validation between outer and inner array dimensions

analysis_questions: Is this function ever called in production?
Are there alternative code paths to add multiple rewards to multiple bribes?
validation_requirements: Test with _token = [[A,B],[C]] and __bribes = [bribe1,bribe2] — confirm revert or wrong behavior
Verify correct fix: k < _token[i].length

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Admin attempts to add different reward tokens to different bribes. The function either reverts (if inner arrays are shorter) or silently skips tokens (if inner arrays are longer), leaving bribes without required reward token registrations.
prerequisites: Function is called with non-uniform inner array lengths

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Operational failure; bribes may not have required reward tokens registered
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Can use addRewardToBribe individually as workaround

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for addRewardsToBribes with varying array dimensions

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Function call reverts with array OOB

threats.title: MinterUpgradeable.calculate_rebase Underflows When smNFT Balance Is Double-Counted
threats.hypothesis_description: calculate_rebase computes veBlackSupply = _veTotal + _smNFTBalance + _superMassiveBonus and blackSupply = _blackTotal + _superMassiveBonus. Since _veTotal (black.balanceOf(ve)) already includes smNFT locked tokens (they are held by the ve contract), adding _smNFTBalance double-counts those tokens. If _veTotal + _smNFTBalance > _blackTotal, then circulatingBlack = blackSupply - veBlackSupply underflows in Solidity 0.8, permanently blocking update_period().
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a critical accounting error that can freeze all protocol emissions. As smNFT adoption grows, the underflow condition becomes more likely. Once triggered, no new epoch can start.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _veTotal = black.balanceOf(ve) includes smNFT locked tokens; _smNFTBalance added again; veBlackSupply can exceed blackSupply causing underflow at line 91

preconditions: smNFT balance grows large enough that _veTotal + _smNFTBalance > _blackTotal
Depends on how smNFT tokens are accounted in ve contract's balanceOf

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: veBlackSupply = _veTotal + _smNFTBalance + bonus; blackSupply = _blackTotal + bonus; circulatingBlack = blackSupply - veBlackSupply
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: If veTotal already includes smNFT locked tokens, double-counting causes veBlackSupply > blackSupply → underflow revert
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No safe subtraction or bounds check on circulatingBlack

analysis_questions: Does ve.balanceOf(ve) include smNFT locked tokens in its total?
What is the current smNFT balance relative to total supply?
At what threshold does the underflow trigger?

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: As protocol grows and more smNFTs are created, the double-counting causes veBlackSupply to exceed blackSupply. update_period() reverts, permanently halting all emissions, fee distributions, and epoch transitions. Protocol is frozen with no recovery path.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade to fix formula

If the factory holds reward tokens to make CL gauge creation work, the seeding transfer becomes an attacker-controlled sink. Even if it reverts when insufficient, it can be used to permanently block CL gauge creation (DoS) if the protocol relies on keeping the factory funded and open.

threats.title: Permissionless GaugeManager.claimRewards(address[]) can be abused to call arbitrary gauge contracts and grief or reenter external systems

threats.title: BribeFactoryV3.recoverERC20AndUpdateData Calls Wrong Function, Causing Bribe Insolvency
threats.hypothesis_description: BribeFactoryV3.recoverERC20AndUpdateData is intended to recover tokens AND update the Bribe's tokenRewardsPerEpoch accounting. However, it calls `IBribe.emergencyRecoverERC20()` instead of `IBribe.recoverERC20AndUpdateData()`. This means tokens are withdrawn but tokenRewardsPerEpoch is NOT decremented. Subsequent claimants will attempt to claim rewards backed by insufficient token balance, causing later claimants' transfers to revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a clear copy-paste bug where the wrong function is called. When admin recovers tokens expecting accounting to be updated, the bribe contract becomes insolvent. Early claimants drain the remaining balance; later claimants get nothing.

threats.title: Bribe.earned Unbounded Loop DoS Permanently Locks User Rewards
threats.hypothesis_description: Bribe.earned() iterates over all unclaimed epochs in a for loop: `for (uint256 i = 0; i < numEpochs; i++)`. With WEEK=1800 seconds (30 minutes per epoch), 48 epochs accumulate per day. If a user doesn't claim bribes for even a moderate period, the loop's SLOAD-heavy iterations (checkpoint binary search per epoch) exceed block gas limits. The user's rewards become permanently unclaimable with no partial claim mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: Each iteration performs getPriorBalanceIndex (binary search through checkpoints - multiple SLOADs) and getPriorSupplyIndex (another binary search). At ~48 epochs/day, 30 days creates 1440 iterations. Even with Avalanche's higher gas limits, this will exceed limits due to the O(n*log(m)) complexity where n=epochs and m=checkpoints.

threats.title: BlackGovernor.clock() Returns Zero Breaking All Governance Functionality
threats.hypothesis_description: BlackGovernor.clock() has an empty implementation returning default uint48(0). This function is used by the inherited OpenZeppelin Governor framework for proposal snapshot timing, voting period calculations, and EIP-6372 compliance. Returning 0 means all proposal snapshots reference timestamp 0, causing getPastVotes lookups to return 0 for all voters, making it impossible to reach quorum on any proposal. Similarly, CLOCK_MODE() returns an empty string, violating EIP-6372. This renders the entire governance system (BlackGovernor → nudge → tailEmissionRate adjustment) permanently non-functional.
threats.investigation_rationale: The governance system is the sole mechanism to adjust tail emission rates via nudge(). If governance cannot execute, the protocol loses its only inflation control mechanism once tail emissions begin.

Any user attempts to create or vote on a governance proposal

threats.title: GaugeManager.distributeFees(CL) withdraws 100% CommunityVault token balances each call; any revert or permission mismatch blocks fee distribution for all pools in batch runs

threats.title: GaugeCL notifyRewardAmount Silent Truncation of Large Rewards
threats.hypothesis_description: In GaugeCL.notifyRewardAmount, the uint256 reward amount is explicitly cast to uint128 when calling algebraEternalFarming.addRewards. If the protocol distributes a reward token with a high supply or high decimals where the reward amount exceeds type(uint128).max (~3.4e38), the value will silently truncate. The full amount is transferred from the GaugeManager, but only the truncated amount is added to the farming pool, causing a massive permanent loss of reward tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: Silent truncation is a dangerous pattern when interacting with external protocols that use smaller integer types. It leads to severe accounting desyncs and trapped funds.

threats.title: GaugeManager.claimBribes Reentrancy via Arbitrary Bribe Address Allows Forced Emission Distribution
threats.hypothesis_description: GaugeManager.claimBribes lacks nonReentrant modifier and accepts arbitrary addresses as bribe contracts without validation. An attacker with a veNFT can pass a malicious contract that calls back into GaugeManager.distributeAll() (which IS nonReentrant-guarded but the guard was never set by claimBribes). This allows atomically triggering emission distribution at a controlled time, potentially after manipulating voting weights in the same transaction.
threats.investigation_rationale: The reentrancy guard (ReentrancyGuardUpgradeable) only protects when the CALLING function sets the lock. Since claimBribes doesn't use nonReentrant, the _status remains _NOT_ENTERED, allowing re-entry into any nonReentrant function on GaugeManager.

threats.title: Black.sol initialMint Creates 500M Tokens Despite 50M Documentation — 10x Supply Inflation
threats.hypothesis_description: Black.sol's initialMint function has a comment stating 'Initial mint: total 50M' but the code executes _mint(_recipient, 500 * 1e6 * 1e18), which mints 500 million tokens — 10x the documented amount. If the protocol's economic model was designed around a 50M initial supply, the actual 500M mint fundamentally breaks tokenomics, emission schedules, rebase calculations, and voting power distribution.

The economic model constants (TAIL_START = 8,969,150, weekly = 10,000,000) may have been calibrated for a different initial supply. A 10x supply inflation would mean emissions represent a much smaller percentage of total supply than intended.

threats.title: MinterUpgradeable._initialize Race Condition Can Block Protocol Permanently or Corrupt Initial Distribution
threats.hypothesis_description: MinterUpgradeable._initialize is a one-shot function gated only by `require(_initializer == msg.sender)`. If the deployer does not call _initialize in the same transaction as deployment, anyone who knows the deployer's address could potentially front-run it. More critically, if _initialize is NEVER called, `_initializer` remains non-zero, and `update_period()` is permanently blocked (it requires `_initializer == address(0)`). This halts all protocol emissions indefinitely.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _initialize function is the critical gating mechanism for the entire emission system. Its one-shot nature and the permanent block if uncalled represent a single point of failure for the protocol.
threats.config_evidence: Deployment script V1/upgradeable/deployMinterUpgradeable.js deploys via proxy but does not call _initialize in the same transaction

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(_initializer == msg.sender) one-shot gate; _initializer = address(0); update_period checks _initializer == address(0)

preconditions: MinterUpgradeable deployed and initialized via proxy
_initialize not yet called
Deployer's address known to attacker (for front-running) OR deployer delays calling _initialize

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: update_period: if(block.timestamp >= _period + WEEK && _initializer == address(0)) — blocked when _initializer != address(0)
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Front-run _initialize with malicious claimants to steal initial token distribution; OR never call _initialize to permanently block emissions
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker receives initial veNFT locks worth entire max parameter; OR protocol halts permanently

threats.title: Any caller can create CL gauges that forcibly redirect Algebra CommunityVault fee receiver to attacker-selected GaugeCL (fee hijack / DoS)
threats.hypothesis_description: GaugeManager.createGauge*() is permissionless and for CL gauges calls changeCommunityFeeReceiver(gauge) on the pool's CommunityVault. If the vault allows this call (or GaugeManager is authorized), any unprivileged user can create a gauge for a target CL pool and force the vault to send community fees to the newly deployed GaugeCL (owned by GaugeManager). This can: (a) redirect fees away from intended receiver, (b) break fee flows if gauge later enters emergency / internal_bribe misconfigured, or (c) enable grief by repeatedly creating gauges across pools (where not yet created) and capturing future fee routing.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete, on-chain, permissionless path that changes an external protocol-critical configuration (community fee receiver) during gauge creation. It must be validated whether AlgebraCommunityVault restricts changeCommunityFeeReceiver and under what roles; if permissive, this is a real fee-routing takeover vector.
deployment_assumptions: Algebra CommunityVault.changeCommunityFeeReceiver is callable by GaugeManager (or permissionless)

TokenHandler whitelist/connector conditions can be satisfied for the pool's token0/token1
threats.deployment_assumptions_note: If CommunityVault restricts changeCommunityFeeReceiver to a privileged vault admin and GaugeManager is not that admin, gauge creation would revert and the fee hijack path fails.
threats.config_evidence: No on-chain deployment evidence in provided scripts about granting GaugeManager a CommunityVault admin/withdrawer role; contract defines COMMUNITY_FEE_WITHDRAWER_ROLE/COMMUNITY_FEE_VAULT_ADMINISTRATOR constants but does not use them in calls.

threats.title: GaugeCL.setInternalBribe Meaningless Validation Allows address(0), Breaking Fee Distribution
threats.hypothesis_description: GaugeCL.setInternalBribe uses `require(_int >= address(0), 'zero')` which is always true since all addresses are unsigned and >= 0. This allows setting internal_bribe to address(0). When _claimFees() subsequently tries to call `IERC20(token).safeApprove(address(0), amount)` or `IBribe(address(0)).notifyRewardAmount()`, the calls will revert, permanently blocking fee distribution for that gauge.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a clear code defect - the validation is semantically wrong. The intended check was `require(_int != address(0))`. While it requires the owner (GaugeManager) to call it, any operational error in bribe migration could trigger this. Once set, fee distribution reverts permanently for the affected gauge.

threats.title: Unbounded referral fee allows 100% LP fee extraction or DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: In `GaugeFactoryCL.sol`, `setReferralFee` allows the admin to set `dibsPercentage` to any value without an upper bound check. In `GaugeCL._claimFees`, the fee sent to the dibs address is calculated as `claimed0 * referralFee / 1000`. threats.title: Meaningless Address Validation Allows Bricking Gauge Fee Distribution
threats.hypothesis_description: The setInternalBribe function in GaugeCL attempts to validate the new internal bribe address using `require(_int >= address(0))`. Because addresses are treated as unsigned integers in Solidity, this condition is always true, even for the zero address. threats.investigation_rationale: Flawed input validation on critical configuration setters can lead to accidental protocol bricking. While it requires an admin mistake, the lack of safeguards makes it a notable operational risk.

threats.title: Read-API ownership setters allow silent data-integrity hijack (misleading pool/NFT info) that can drive incorrect operational actions
threats.hypothesis_description: Several API/helper contracts implement their own `owner` and allow changing critical pointers (voter, gaugeManager, factories, NFPM) via `setOwner`/`setVoter`/`setFactory`/`setNonfungiblePositionManager` without OZ Ownable patterns. If the API proxy admin/owner is misconfigured or compromised, attackers can repoint these APIs to malicious contracts returning fake pool/NFT data. This can mislead operators/automation into interacting with wrong gaugeManager/voter addresses, including calling epoch flip scripts or bribe/fee flows incorrectly.
threats.investigation_rationale: While these are 'read APIs', they are commonly consumed by dashboards and ops tooling. Misconfiguration or compromise can create real operational losses (wrong contract addresses used, wrong pools targeted), especially on L2-like environments where users rely on trusted endpoints.
deployment_assumptions: These API contracts are used by official UI/ops tooling as a source of truth
Owner key/proxy admin is not secured or is overly permissive
Consumers do not validate against on-chain canonical sources
threats.config_evidence: Deployment scripts show proxies deployed for AlgebraPoolAPI; no evidence of ownership transfer/hardening or restricted admin usage in provided scripts.

proof_of_signal.pattern: custom owner variable + require(msg.sender == owner) gating; setters can repoint core dependencies (voter/factory/NFPM/gaugeManager)

threats.title: EpochController initialization/deployment gaps can leave performUpkeep paths unusable or abusable by mis-set registries
threats.hypothesis_description: EpochController.initialize does not set genesisManager, and performUpkeep/performPreUpkeep depend on automationRegistry/automationRegistry2 and genesisManager being correctly configured. If owner misconfigures registries or forgets to set genesisManager, epoch flips revert and emissions/fees distribution halt. If owner sets automationRegistry to an unintended address, that address can call upkeep functions (subject to checkUpkeep conditions) potentially at undesired times, increasing operational fragility.
threats.investigation_rationale: Although owner-controlled, this is a recurring real-world failure mode: missing wiring causes systemic halts. Scripts show registries being set post-deploy, but no script evidence shows genesisManager setup before epoch flips.
deployment_assumptions: genesisManager is not set before the first upkeep calls
automationRegistry/automationRegistry2 can be set incorrectly or left unset in production
